title: Which version of the flood the museum keeps
summary: Reflect on how a regional museum, forced to choose a single account of a disaster to exhibit, inevitably canonises one community's memory and quietens another's — and whether that choice can ever be made innocently.
topic: Who decides which version of a history survives in a museum
prompt: Write a 600–800-word reflective essay on the curator's dilemma when a single disaster is remembered differently by the people who lived through it. Ground it in one concrete case (real or plausible), trace how curatorial choices privilege one memory over another, and resist a tidy resolution. Use hedging, nominalization, and at least one complex concession.
hintStarters: Not until I stood in front of the exhibit's single, authoritative caption did it occur to me that…
What the curator presents as neutral selection is, on closer inspection,…
It would be too easy to accuse the museum of bad faith; the harder truth is that…

sampleText:
Not until I stood in front of the exhibit's single, authoritative caption did it occur to me that a museum cannot remember a flood; it can only choose whose memory of the flood to keep. The town was a small one on the Vistula, and the water had come in 1934, taking eleven houses and, depending on whom you asked, somewhere between four and nine lives. The caption settled on six. I have no reason to think the figure was dishonest. What unsettled me was the quiet confidence of the wording — the way a number arrived at through guesswork had been given the bearing of fact, simply by being printed on the wall.

The curator I later spoke with did not pretend the choice had been easy. She had three sources. The parish register, meticulous and partial, recorded the deaths it recognised as deaths — which is to say, the baptised, the settled, the locally accountable. The municipal report, written by an engineer with a career to protect, foregrounded the failure of an upstream sluice and relegated the human cost to a closing paragraph. And then there were the descendants of the seasonal labourers who had been camped on the floodplain that spring, whose account survived only as oral testimony, passed down and therefore, by the museum's own standards of provenance, the least authenticatable of the three. To exhibit all of them in full would have meant a wall of contradictions. To exhibit one meant deciding which contradiction the town could live with.

It would be too easy to accuse the museum of bad faith. The harder truth is that selection is not an optional sin a more scrupulous institution might avoid; it is the condition of exhibiting anything at all. A glass case holds what it holds. A caption is, by its nature, shorter than the event. Even the decision to present three accounts side by side — the curatorial gesture we now reflexively praise as honest — is itself a frame, one that quietly implies the three are commensurable, equally worth weighing, when the people who lived the flood would have insisted they were nothing of the kind.

What the curator presents as neutral selection is, on closer inspection, an act with consequences for the living. The labourers' descendants had asked, modestly, that the camp on the floodplain be named in the wall text. It was not. The omission was not malicious; the engineer's report had not mentioned the camp, and the parish register could not, since the camp's dead were not its parishioners. The museum, in deferring to its more authenticatable sources, had reproduced the very hierarchy that had let those deaths go uncounted the first time. Institutional authority, applied conscientiously, had laundered an old erasure into a clean and defensible exhibit.

I do not think the answer is to abandon judgement and display everything, as though a museum of undifferentiated rumour would be more truthful than one that chooses. A record that refuses to distinguish the better-evidenced from the worse is not humble; it is merely useless. Yet the case suggests something the profession is reluctant to say aloud: that the standards by which we decide what counts as evidence are not innocent of power, and that the communities best at producing documents are rarely the communities most in need of being remembered. The seasonal labourers left no register because no one thought their lives worth registering — and that same silence then disqualifies their memory in the present.

What would epistemic humility look like here? Not, I think, a grand confession printed on the wall. Something smaller. The camp could have been named, with a candid note that the figure was uncertain and the sources in tension. The caption could have admitted its own selectiveness rather than performing the bearing of fact. None of this would resolve the dilemma; nothing resolves it. But it would refuse the one move I find hardest to forgive — the move by which a guess becomes a finding, a finding becomes the record, and the record quietly forgets that anyone was ever left out. To preserve a history is, unavoidably, to decide which version of it survives. The least we can do is decline to pretend we did not decide.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Inversion after 'Not until…' for delayed realisation
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu mở đầu 'Not until I stood in front of the exhibit's single, authoritative caption did it occur to me that…' đảo ngữ trợ động từ 'did' lên trước chủ ngữ giả 'it' vì mệnh đề trạng ngữ phủ định 'Not until…' đứng đầu câu. Cấu trúc này nhấn mạnh thời điểm nhận ra muộn màng và là dấu ấn của văn xuôi C1 trang trọng.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: The opening 'Not until I stood in front of the exhibit's single, authoritative caption did it occur to me that…' inverts the auxiliary 'did' before the dummy subject 'it' because the negative time-clause 'Not until…' fronts the sentence. The structure dramatises a delayed realisation and is a hallmark of formal C1 prose.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Nominalization that turns process into agent
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Cụm 'an act of erasure' và động từ-thành-danh-từ 'selection' (trong 'selection is not an optional sin') biến hành động thành chủ thể có thể lập luận trực tiếp. Tương tự, 'the omission', 'displacement' và 'redress' đều là danh hoá, cho phép tác giả gán hậu quả và trách nhiệm cho chính quá trình — đặc trưng văn phong học thuật C1.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: The verb-to-noun shift in 'selection is not an optional sin' lets a process become the subject of an argument, as does 'the omission was not malicious'. Such nominalizations allow the writer to attribute consequence and accountability to the process itself — a defining feature of C1 academic style.

sampleTranslationVi:
Mãi đến khi đứng trước dòng chú thích đơn lẻ, đầy quyền uy của triển lãm, tôi mới nhận ra rằng một bảo tàng không thể ghi nhớ một trận lụt; nó chỉ có thể chọn giữ lại ký ức của ai về trận lụt đó. Thị trấn là một nơi nhỏ bên sông Vistula, và nước đã tràn vào năm 1934, cuốn đi mười một ngôi nhà và, tuỳ người bạn hỏi, đâu đó từ bốn đến chín mạng người. Dòng chú thích chốt ở con số sáu. Tôi không có lý do gì để cho rằng con số ấy là gian dối. Điều khiến tôi bất an là sự tự tin lặng lẽ trong cách diễn đạt — cách một con số đến từ phỏng đoán lại được khoác lên dáng vẻ của sự thật, chỉ đơn giản bằng việc được in lên tường.

Người phụ trách mà sau này tôi trò chuyện không hề giả vờ rằng lựa chọn đó là dễ dàng. Bà có ba nguồn. Sổ giáo xứ, tỉ mỉ nhưng phiến diện, ghi lại những cái chết mà nó công nhận là cái chết — tức là những người đã rửa tội, đã định cư, được tính sổ tại địa phương. Báo cáo của thành phố, do một kỹ sư có sự nghiệp cần bảo vệ viết ra, làm nổi bật sự cố của cống xả thượng nguồn và đẩy tổn thất con người xuống một đoạn cuối. Rồi còn có hậu duệ của những người lao động thời vụ từng cắm trại trên vùng ngập lụt mùa xuân đó, mà lời kể chỉ tồn tại dưới dạng chứng từ truyền miệng, được truyền lại và do đó, theo chính tiêu chuẩn về nguồn gốc của bảo tàng, là khó xác thực nhất trong ba nguồn. Trưng bày đầy đủ cả ba sẽ nghĩa là một bức tường đầy mâu thuẫn. Trưng bày một nghĩa là quyết định thị trấn có thể sống chung với mâu thuẫn nào.

Sẽ quá dễ dàng khi buộc tội bảo tàng hành xử thiếu thiện chí. Sự thật khó nuốt hơn là: sự chọn lọc không phải một tội lỗi tuỳ chọn mà một định chế cẩn trọng hơn có thể tránh; đó là điều kiện tiên quyết để trưng bày bất cứ thứ gì. Một tủ kính chỉ chứa được những gì nó chứa. Một dòng chú thích, theo bản chất, ngắn hơn chính sự kiện. Ngay cả quyết định trình bày ba lời kể cạnh nhau — cử chỉ trưng bày mà ngày nay ta theo phản xạ ca ngợi là trung thực — bản thân cũng là một khung diễn giải, một khung lặng lẽ ngụ ý rằng ba nguồn đó có thể so sánh ngang nhau, đáng cân nhắc như nhau, trong khi những người đã sống qua trận lụt hẳn sẽ khăng khăng rằng chúng chẳng hề như vậy.

Điều mà người phụ trách trình bày như một sự chọn lọc trung lập, khi xem kỹ, là một hành động có hậu quả với người đang sống. Hậu duệ của những người lao động đã xin, một cách khiêm tốn, rằng khu trại trên vùng ngập lụt được nêu tên trong phần chữ trên tường. Điều đó đã không xảy ra. Sự bỏ sót không phải ác ý; báo cáo của kỹ sư đã không nhắc đến khu trại, và sổ giáo xứ thì không thể, vì người chết của khu trại không phải giáo dân của nó. Bảo tàng, khi nhường quyền cho các nguồn dễ xác thực hơn của mình, đã tái tạo chính thứ bậc đã khiến những cái chết đó không được đếm đến ngay từ đầu. Thẩm quyền của định chế, được áp dụng tận tâm, đã tẩy trắng một sự xoá bỏ cũ thành một triển lãm sạch sẽ và có thể bào chữa.

Tôi không nghĩ câu trả lời là từ bỏ phán đoán và trưng bày mọi thứ, như thể một bảo tàng toàn tin đồn không phân biệt sẽ trung thực hơn một bảo tàng biết chọn. Một hồ sơ từ chối phân biệt cái có bằng chứng tốt hơn với cái kém hơn thì không hề khiêm tốn; nó chỉ vô dụng. Thế nhưng trường hợp này gợi ra điều mà ngành nghề này ngại nói thành lời: rằng những tiêu chuẩn ta dùng để quyết định cái gì là bằng chứng không hề vô can với quyền lực, và rằng những cộng đồng giỏi tạo ra tài liệu hiếm khi là những cộng đồng cần được ghi nhớ nhất. Những người lao động thời vụ không để lại sổ sách vì chẳng ai nghĩ đời họ đáng được ghi vào sổ — và chính sự im lặng đó rồi lại loại bỏ tư cách ký ức của họ trong hiện tại.

Sự khiêm tốn về nhận thức ở đây sẽ trông như thế nào? Tôi nghĩ, không phải là một lời thú nhận hùng hồn in lên tường. Một điều gì đó nhỏ hơn. Khu trại lẽ ra có thể được nêu tên, kèm một ghi chú thẳng thắn rằng con số là không chắc chắn và các nguồn mâu thuẫn nhau. Dòng chú thích lẽ ra có thể thừa nhận tính chọn lọc của chính nó thay vì trình diễn dáng vẻ của sự thật. Không điều nào trong số đó giải quyết được tình thế lưỡng nan; chẳng có gì giải quyết được nó cả. Nhưng nó sẽ từ chối cái động thái mà tôi thấy khó tha thứ nhất — động thái mà nhờ đó một phỏng đoán trở thành một phát hiện, một phát hiện trở thành hồ sơ chính thức, và hồ sơ lặng lẽ quên rằng từng có ai đó bị bỏ ra ngoài. Bảo tồn một lịch sử, không thể tránh khỏi, là quyết định phiên bản nào của nó sẽ sống sót. Điều tối thiểu ta có thể làm là từ chối giả vờ rằng mình đã không hề quyết định.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence demonstrates correct inversion after a fronted negative time-clause?
options: Not until I stood in front of the caption it occurred to me that the choice was political.
Not until I stood in front of the caption did it occur to me that the choice was political.
Not until I stood in front of the caption occurred it to me that the choice was political.
Not until I had stood in front of the caption that it occurred to me the choice was political.
mcQuestions.explanation: A fronted 'Not until…' clause forces subject-auxiliary inversion in the main clause: 'did it occur', not 'it occurred'.
mcQuestions.hint: Negative time-clause at the front — flip the auxiliary and the subject in the main clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, why is selection 'not an optional sin a more scrupulous institution might avoid'?
options: Because all curators are secretly biased against minorities.
Because museums lack the budget to display every account.
Because exhibiting anything at all requires choosing, since a case and a caption are necessarily smaller than the event.
Because visitors prefer a single clear story to several confusing ones.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues selection is the very condition of display: 'A glass case holds what it holds. A caption is, by its nature, shorter than the event.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look at what the essay says is the 'condition of exhibiting anything at all'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay mean by saying the museum 'had laundered an old erasure into a clean and defensible exhibit'?
options: The museum physically cleaned old flood-damaged documents.
The museum fabricated documents to support its preferred story.
The museum removed offensive material to avoid controversy.
By relying on its most authenticatable sources, the museum repeated and legitimised an original act of omission.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Laundered' here is metaphorical: conscientious procedure reproduced the same hierarchy that had let the labourers' deaths go uncounted, giving the old erasure a respectable new form.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Launder' implies making something dubious look legitimate.

mcQuestions.prompt: Why could the parish register not record the camp's dead?
options: Because the camp's dead were not parishioners, and the register only recorded those it recognised as locally accountable.
Because the register was destroyed in the flood.
Because the priest deliberately concealed the deaths.
Because the engineer ordered the names removed.
mcQuestions.explanation: The register recorded 'the baptised, the settled, the locally accountable' — by definition excluding the seasonal labourers camped on the floodplain.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at who the register counted as belonging to the parish.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option is a nominalization, the C1 feature highlighted in the grammar notes?
options: to gloss over
the omission
well-intentioned
passed down
mcQuestions.explanation: 'The omission' turns the verb 'omit' into a noun that can act as the subject of an argument — a defining C1 nominalization.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for a noun derived from a verb that names a process.

mcQuestions.prompt: The essay rejects the idea of 'displaying everything'. Why?
options: Because it would offend the descendants of the labourers.
Because museums are legally required to verify all claims.
Because a record that refuses to distinguish better-evidenced from worse accounts is not humble but merely useless.
Because oral testimony is always unreliable.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay says abandoning judgement entirely produces 'a museum of undifferentiated rumour' that is 'not humble; it is merely useless'.
mcQuestions.hint: The writer distinguishes humility from refusing to judge at all.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best captures the essay's complex concession?
options: Museums are biased, so they should be abolished.
There are pros and cons to displaying one account versus several.
Curators should always defer to oral testimony over written records.

I do not think the answer is to abandon judgement and display everything … Yet the case suggests something the profession is reluctant to say aloud: that the standards by which we decide what counts as evidence are not innocent of power.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C grants the opposing position fully (don't display everything), then uses 'Yet…' to introduce a narrowed, qualified counter-claim — the structure of a complex C1 concession.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for a full grant of the objection followed by a restricting 'Yet…'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the best cohesion device to follow the sentence 'The omission was not malicious'?
options: The engineer's report had not mentioned the camp, and the parish register could not — so the museum simply reproduced an older silence.
Also, the museum had a gift shop near the exit.
Malicious means having intent to harm someone.
In today's museums, omissions happen all the time for many reasons.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B uses a causal chain ('had not mentioned … could not … so') that explains the non-malicious omission and links it to the essay's argument about reproduced silence; the others are off-topic, definitional, or vague filler.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option explains WHY the omission happened and ties back to the argument?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'epistemic humility' refer to in the essay?
options: A grand public apology printed on the museum wall.
Small, candid gestures — naming the camp, admitting the figure was uncertain and sources in tension — that acknowledge the limits of what is known.
Refusing to display anything until every fact is verified.
Letting visitors vote on which account is true.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay explicitly says epistemic humility would be 'something smaller' — naming the camp and admitting uncertainty, not a grand confession.
mcQuestions.hint: The writer contrasts a 'grand confession' with 'something smaller'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the rhetorical point of 'a guess becomes a finding, a finding becomes the record'?
options: It praises the rigour of the museum's documentation process.
It argues that all findings are guesses and therefore worthless.
It traces, through escalating stages, how uncertain information is gradually hardened into authoritative fact and the original doubt forgotten.
It describes the legal process of authenticating an artefact.
mcQuestions.explanation: The chained clauses dramatise a creeping certainty: each step launders the uncertainty of the last until 'the record quietly forgets that anyone was ever left out'.
mcQuestions.hint: Notice the escalating chain and where it ends.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence is grammatical, on-topic, and matches the essay's reflective register?
options: The museum totally messed up by leaving the camp guys out of the story.
It is a well-known fact that museums always lie to the public about everything.
Pursuant to curatorial protocol, the exhibit's evidentiary basis was duly authenticated.
What unsettled me was the quiet confidence of the wording — the way a guess had been given the bearing of fact.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B is reflective and precise; A is too casual, C overstates with a sweeping falsehood, and D is wrongly bureaucratic for a personal essay.
mcQuestions.hint: Match the essay's thoughtful, first-person register — not slang, not legalese, not absolutist.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which paraphrase is closest to 'the communities best at producing documents are rarely the communities most in need of being remembered'?
options: The people with the records to prove their history are usually not the people history has neglected — so evidence and need pull in opposite directions.
Well-documented communities deserve more attention than poorly documented ones.
Every community should learn to keep better written records.
Memory is unimportant compared with documentary evidence.
mcQuestions.explanation: The line argues evidentiary capacity and moral need are inversely related; option B captures that tension, while the others invert or miss the point.
mcQuestions.hint: Notice that the sentence sets documentary ability against need to be remembered.

mcQuestions.prompt: Why does the essay describe 'three accounts side by side' as 'itself a frame'?
options: Because side-by-side displays require expensive glass cases.
Because presenting them as comparable quietly implies they are equally weighable, which the people who lived the flood would deny.
Because three is too many accounts for visitors to read.
Because oral and written sources should never be shown together.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay notes that even the 'honest' gesture of three accounts imposes a frame — 'implies the three are commensurable' — and so is not neutral either.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at what showing accounts as equals quietly assumes.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which word best fills the gap, matching C1 register? 'The museum, in ___ to its more authenticatable sources, reproduced an old hierarchy.'
options: giving up
looking
deferring
bossing
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Deferring to' (yielding authority to) is the precise, formal collocation; the others are wrong register or wrong meaning.
mcQuestions.hint: Which verb means 'yielding authority to' and collocates with 'to its sources'?

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the function of the final sentence, 'The least we can do is decline to pretend we did not decide'?
options: It offers a complete solution that resolves the curator's dilemma.
It blames individual curators for systemic problems.
It recommends that museums stop selecting altogether.
It refuses false resolution — accepting that selection is unavoidable while insisting the act of choosing be acknowledged rather than disguised.
mcQuestions.explanation: The closing holds the paradox open: it does not solve the dilemma but asks only that the inevitable choosing not be hidden behind the 'bearing of fact'.
mcQuestions.hint: The essay said nothing 'resolves' the dilemma — so what is the modest demand it ends on?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option preserves the hedged tone the prompt requires?
options: The case suggests something the profession is reluctant to say aloud.
Museums always erase the poor on purpose.
It is obvious that the curator acted in bad faith.
Nobody in a museum has ever told the truth.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Suggests' and 'reluctant to say' hedge the claim appropriately; the other options are absolute or accusatory, breaking the essay's careful register.
mcQuestions.hint: Hedging means softening the claim — look for tentative verbs.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of an event in your own family or community that different people remember differently. If you had to choose one version to write down for the future, whose memory would you privilege, what would you lose, and how would you mark the loss?
